snippets.title: Creating basic matrices with NumPy
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

# 2 x 3 matrix
A = np.array([
    [1, 2, 3],
    [4, 5, 6]
])

# Zero matrix (3 x 4)
Z = np.zeros((3, 4))

# Identity matrix (4 x 4)
I = np.eye(4)

print("A shape:", A.shape)   # (2, 3)
print("Z shape:", Z.shape)   # (3, 4)
print("I shape:", I.shape)   # (4, 4)
snippets.explanation: NumPy uses the shape attribute to store (rows, columns). Identity and zero matrices are convenient building blocks in linear algebra code.

snippets.title: Matrix addition and scalar multiplication
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

A = np.array([
    [1, 2],
    [3, 4]
])
B = np.array([
    [5, 6],
    [7, 8]
])

# Same shape, so entrywise addition works
C = A + B

# Scalar multiplication multiplies every entry
D = 0.5 * A

print("A:\n", A)
print("B:\n", B)
print("A + B:\n", C)
print("0.5 * A:\n", D)
snippets.explanation: When two matrices have the same shape, NumPy adds them entrywise. Multiplying by a scalar rescales every entry; this matches the algebraic definitions.

snippets.title: Transpose and shape changes
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

X = np.array([
    [1, 2, 3],  # row 0
    [4, 5, 6]   # row 1
])

print("X shape:", X.shape)      # (2, 3)
print("X:\n", X)

XT = X.T
print("X.T shape:", XT.shape)   # (3, 2)
print("X.T:\n", XT)
snippets.explanation: The transpose swaps rows and columns. In NumPy, .T is the standard way to take the transpose of a 2D array.

snippets.title: Shape mismatch mistake in addition
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

A = np.ones((2, 3))
B = np.ones((3, 2))

print("A shape:", A.shape)
print("B shape:", B.shape)

try:
    C = A + B
except ValueError as e:
    print("Error when adding A and B:", e)
snippets.explanation: This snippet shows how NumPy raises an error when you try to add matrices of incompatible shapes. In algebra, A + B is undefined in this case.
